dungeon_master_guide.title: Dungeon Master Fundamentals
dungeon_master_guide.overview: This guide covers the basics of being a Dungeon Master, including roles, responsibilities, tips, and essential requirements for running a D&D game.
dungeon_master_guide.core_principle: The most important part of being a good DM is facilitating the fun of everyone at the table.

dm_roles.title: What Does a DM Do?
dm_roles.description: A Dungeon Master wears many hats and plays multiple important roles during a game

roles.description: The DM plays monsters and all NPCs encountered
responsibilities: Choose monster actions and roll dice for their attacks
Portray various personalities and voices for NPCs
Bring creatures and characters to life through roleplay

roles.description: Like a movie director, the DM designs encounters and paces the story
responsibilities: Decide what characters encounter during adventures
Describe the scenes and situations presented to players
Control the pace of play sessions
Create situations that facilitate fun for everyone

roles.description: The DM adapts to player actions and applies rules creatively
responsibilities: Apply rules on the fly as situations arise
Imagine consequences of character actions
Adapt the adventure based on player choices
Keep the game fun while maintaining consistency

roles.description: When clarity is needed, the DM interprets and applies rules
responsibilities: Make rulings when rule interpretation is unclear
Ensure consistent application of rules
Make fair decisions that support fun gameplay

roles.description: The DM crafts and narrates the adventure narrative
responsibilities: Create adventures with compelling plots
Set situations that entice exploration and interaction
Develop the narrative arc of the campaign
Create memorable moments and encounters

roles.description: The DM often teaches new players how to play
responsibilities: Explain rules to new players
Help players understand mechanics
Guide character creation process
Make the learning experience enjoyable

roles.description: The DM creates the world and setting for adventures
responsibilities: Build the campaign world or adapt published settings
Create nations, cities, and locations
Establish lore and history
Make the world feel alive and responsive

dm_tips.title: DM Tips for Success
dm_tips.description: Essential guidance for running a successful D&D game

tips.description: D&D is fundamentally about telling a story as a group
guidance: Let other players contribute through their character actions
Encourage player engagement and roleplay
Ask players what their characters are doing
Value player input and creativity
Balance DM narrative with player agency

tips.description: The DM is not competing against the players
guidance: Your role is to provide fun challenges, not to defeat the party
Design encounters that test players without crushing them
Celebrate player victories and clever solutions
Focus on collaborative storytelling rather than DM vs Players

tips.description: Treat all players fairly while remaining flexible with rules
guidance: Apply rules impartially to all characters
Make decisions that ensure everyone has fun
Use rules as guidance, not iron-clad law
Consider player agency and narrative flow
Be consistent in rulings within sessions

tips.description: Open communication prevents and solves most problems
guidance: Have honest conversations about expectations
Ask players for feedback after or between sessions
Discuss tone, content, and game style before starting
Address issues promptly and directly
Create safe space for players to voice concerns

tips.description: Perfection is not required or expected
guidance: Correct mistakes when you notice them and move forward
No one expects you to memorize every rule
If you realize a mistake after the session, acknowledge it at the start of the next one
Make adjustments moving forward
Learn from mistakes without dwelling on them

optimal_size.notes: D&D plays best with this configuration, but it's possible to run games with fewer or more adventurers

dm_role_sharing.description: Some groups rotate the DM role or share it among multiple players
considerations: Others may feel trapped as the 'forever DM'
Each DM brings unique style to the game
dm_role_sharing.note: See Group Size section in Dungeon Master's Guide for possibilities

things_you_need.title: Essential Requirements for Playing
things_you_need.description: The minimum requirements for running a D&D game have remained largely unchanged since 1974

essentials.description: One player takes on the special role of DM
Other players take on roles of heroes (characters/adventurers)
essentials.note: Each player should have a character sheet and dice

essentials.description: Physical or virtual space where everyone can gather
essentials.minimum: Room for entire group to participate
considerations: Advance reservation if space is shared
Can be in-person or online (video call, virtual tabletop)

essentials.description: Regular time when group can meet
variability: Some groups play for a few hours weekly
Others play for a full day once a month
Schedule that works for your group
for_new_groups: Allows people to try D&D with low commitment
Success can lead to longer-term commitment

essentials.description: Complete set of dice needed for game resolution
essentials.quantity: At least 2 of each kind recommended
essentials.ideal: Each player has their own full set

essentials.description: Methods for tracking information during play
Comprehensive tracking system for DM planning

suggestions.details: Many stores host D&D events and maintain player networks
suggestions.resource: Store Locator on Wizards of the Coast website

suggestions.details: Friends, family, community members, work colleagues who enjoy gaming or fantasy
Organized groups at schools and universities
Online communities and platforms for finding gaming groups
Local and regional conventions often feature gaming events

playing_space.title: Choosing a Playing Space
playing_space.minimum_requirement: Room for everyone in your group to gather and participate
selection_process: Enlist players' help in choosing space
Think about accessibility needs of all participants
accessibility_considerations: Low light sensitivity (ensure adequate lighting options)
Scent sensitivity (be aware of strong odors)
ideal_characteristics: Reserved in advance if space is shared
online_options: Screen sharing with tools for maps

useful_additions.title: Optional Resources to Enhance Your Game
useful_additions.description: Various resources can enrich the game experience, with many having digital versions

additions.purpose: Shield books, notes, and die rolls from players
online_equivalent: Reference windows for quick information access

content_types: Complete adventures with hooks, plots, maps, encounters
additions.note: D&D playable without these, but many find them valuable additions

grid_options: Vinyl wet-erase mat with printed grid

additions.description: Software designed to facilitate online D&D
capabilities: Fog of war and lighting control
Show players exactly what they can see
Simple screen sharing with drawing programs
considerations: Use whatever works for your group
Some DMs are comfortable with basic screen sharing

encounter_design_introduction.title: Encounter Design Philosophy
core_concepts: Encounters should challenge without crushing the party
Balance difficulty to party level and composition
Use Challenge Rating as a guide
encounter_design_introduction.note: See Encounter Design rules for detailed mechanics

session_management_introduction.title: Running Effective Sessions
session_management_introduction.note: See Session Management rules for detailed guidance

every_dm_is_unique.title: Every DM Is Unique
every_dm_is_unique.overview: No two DMs run the game in exactly the same way—and that's how it should be
every_dm_is_unique.core_principle: You'll be most successful as a DM if you choose a play style that works best for you and your players

rule_of_fun.title: The Rule of Fun
rule_of_fun.core_rule: D&D is a game, and everyone should have fun playing it
rule_of_fun.shared_responsibility: Everyone shares equal responsibility in moving the game along and contributing to fun
how_to_maintain_fun: Treat each other with respect and consideration
Talk through disagreements among players or their characters
Remember that arguments or mean-spirited squabbles get in the way of fun

defining_fun.principle: People have many different ideas about what makes D&D fun
defining_fun.correct_way: The 'right way' to play D&D is the way you and your players agree to and enjoy

success_factors: Everyone comes to the table prepared to contribute
Willingness to adapt for group enjoyment

play_style.title: Defining Your Play Style
play_style.overview: Questions to help you define your unique DM style and the kind of game you want to run

dimensions.dimension: Hack and Slash or Immersive Roleplaying?
dimensions.description: Does the game focus on combat and action or on a rich story with detailed NPCs?
considerations: Treasure and tactics vs character development

dimensions.description: Is the game for all ages, or does it involve mature themes?
Do you prefer gritty realism, or are you more focused on making the game feel cinematic and superheroic?
Do you want to maintain a serious tone, or is humor your goal?
Do you like to plan thoroughly, or do you prefer to improvise?
Is the game a mixture of themes and genres, or does it center on a particular theme or genre such as horror?
considerations: Campaign focus (dungeon crawl, mystery, exploration, etc.)

dimensions.description: Are you comfortable with moral ambiguity, such as allowing the characters to explore whether the end justifies the means? Or are you happier with straightforward heroic principles?

house_rules.title: House Rules
house_rules.definition: New or modified rules you add to your game to make it your own and enhance your desired style

decision_process.question: Will the rule or change improve the game?
decision_process.consideration: Does it address a problem, add fun, or enhance your style?

decision_process.question: Will my players like it?
decision_process.consideration: Will players enjoy this change? Have you asked them?

guidance: If confident both answers are yes, give the new rule a try
Present house rules as experiments
Ask players for feedback on them
If a house rule isn't fun, remove or revise it
Iterate based on actual play experience
best_practices: Explain the reasoning behind new rules
Be willing to rescind or modify
Document all house rules for reference

recording_rules_interpretations.title: Recording Rules Interpretations
recording_rules_interpretations.when_to_record: When a question about rule interpretation comes up in your game
what_to_do: Record how you decide to interpret it
Add it to your collection of house rules
Make it available for reference later
purpose: Helps new players understand your game
recording_rules_interpretations.note: This is effectively a house rule created by actual play situations

atmosphere.title: Creating Atmosphere
atmosphere.overview: Various techniques can enhance the atmosphere and mood of your game sessions

music.description: Many DMs use music to create appropriate atmosphere
music.consideration: Some players find music distracting—check with your group

examples: Dim lighting for mystery or horror
lighting.consideration: Some players have difficulty with low light or specific lighting effects

sound_effects.description: Sound effects can punctuate moments and enhance immersion
sound_effects.consideration: Some players prefer not to be startled by loud noises

visual_aids.description: Visual elements help players visualize events

atmosphere.important_note: Always check with your players about preferences for music, lighting, or sound effects. Ask about accessibility needs and preferences before using these elements

delegation.title: Delegation - Sharing the Load
delegation.overview: You don't have to handle everything yourself as a DM
delegation.principle: Assign tasks to players who enjoy them

examples.reason: If you don't want to break narrative stride looking up rules
examples.benefit: Keeps the game moving while ensuring accuracy

examples.reason: If you don't like tracking Initiative
examples.benefit: Frees you to focus on narration and encounter management

examples.reason: If math slows down your play

examples.benefit: Lets you focus on running the encounter

benefits: Reduces your workload
Increases player engagement
Lets players help in ways they enjoy
Keeps game moving faster

learning_by_observing.title: Learning by Observing Other DMs
learning_by_observing.overview: One of the best ways to learn is to observe other DMs in action
benefits: Provides solid foundation for understanding the role
Inspires cool techniques you can use in your games
Shows different approaches and styles
Demonstrates best practices

questions: How did the DM start the session?
Was there a recap?
How did they set the tone?
What gestures did the DM use when describing a scene?
How did body language change when playing different NPCs?
Did movement enhance or distract?
Did the DM use different voices or mannerisms for NPCs?
Did the DM change pitch or tempo in different situations?
How did voice work enhance characterization?
Did players participate in world-building?
Did players make decisions that sent adventure in unexpected directions?
How did the DM handle player agency?
To what extent did the DM lean on rules?
Did the DM adjudicate situations wisely?
Did adjudication make the game fun?
How much session time was combat vs exploration vs social?
Was the balance appropriate for the adventure?
Did the DM balance all three pillars?
How would you describe the game's tone and mood?
Did it change over the course of the session?
How did the DM manage tone shifts?
Were there words or bits of narration you really liked?
What made those descriptions effective?
How can you apply similar techniques?
What elements of the DM's world grabbed your attention?
How did they establish setting and atmosphere?
What world-building techniques could you use?

ensuring_fun_for_all.title: Ensuring Fun for All
ensuring_fun_for_all.overview: Creating an inclusive, safe, and enjoyable experience at the table
ensuring_fun_for_all.foundation: Discuss with your players the experience you're all hoping for before the game starts

pre_game_discussion.discussion: What experience are everyone hoping for from this game?
What themes and tone will the campaign have?
What topics, themes, and behavior might spoil someone's enjoyment?

mutual_respect.title: Mutual Respect
mutual_respect.overview: Foundation for creating a safe and enjoyable gaming environment
mutual_respect.importance: Whether playing with long-time friends or strangers, create a foundation of mutual trust
mutual_respect.best_conditions: The best games happen when everyone at the table feels safe enough to be themselves, speak up, and get into character
responsibility: Everyone must uphold principles of respect
Difficult conversations often fall on the DM to lead
But everyone has a stake in helping resolve issues
If one player's behavior interferes with others' enjoyment, it's everyone's concern
creating_safety: Assume good intent but address impact

setting_expectations.title: Setting Expectations with Players
setting_expectations.overview: Clear communication prevents misunderstandings and helps attract compatible players
what_to_pitch: The adventures you're thinking about running
what_to_share: Flavors of fantasy that interest you
Play style dimensions relevant to your campaign
benefits: Prepares players to imagine character types
Launches conversations about content to embrace/avoid
Allows players to decide if they want to play
Best to know before play begins if players aren't aligned
importance: Being transparent with players lets them decide if this is their game
Take players' opinions and desires seriously
Ensure they take yours equally seriously
Find a style of play that suits everyone

hard_and_soft_limits.title: Hard and Soft Limits
hard_and_soft_limits.overview: Having conversations about sensitive topics and boundaries before and during the campaign

definitions.definition: Topic that should be handled carefully, as it might create unwelcome anxiety, fear, or discomfort
definitions.handling: Incorporate with care and be ready to veer away if needed

definitions.definition: Topic that should not be mentioned or described

awareness: DMs and players can have phobias or triggers others might not be aware of
Hard limits must be avoided to keep players feeling safe
Soft limits can be included if players consent and are comfortable
core_gameplay_elements: D&D is a game with in-world conflicts and mayhem
Taking damage is core to the game and hard to avoid
Character death happens from time to time (though game has ways to counteract/avoid it)
Some limits can't be worked around easily

communicating_limits.title: Communicating Limits with Players
communicating_limits.overview: Making sure limits discussion is comfortable and clear for everyone
comfort_considerations: Not all players want to discuss limits aloud
Some are new to RPGs or haven't spent time with the group
Nervousness about expressing personal boundaries is common
anonymous_approach: Provide way for players to share limits anonymously
Use anonymous survey or tracking sheet
Reduces pressure of speaking aloud about sensitive topics
compilation_and_sharing: Compile limits into a shared list
Everyone in the group must respect all limits
timing: Have discussion at start of campaign
Revisit when new players join group
Discuss during story or tone shifts
Be willing to update the list as needed

shifting_limits.title: Shifting and Updating Limits
shifting_limits.overview: Limits can evolve during play; make adjustments collaboratively
shifting_limits.principle: Trust that players know their needs best
player_responsibility: Players should bring new limits to DM privately or in the moment
Encourage players to speak up about comfort levels
Players can discover new limits as campaign unfolds
dm_responsibility: Add communicated limits to the list
Check in regularly with the group

limits_in_play.title: Limits During Active Play
limits_in_play.overview: Have agreed-upon signal to communicate when a limit is crossed
limits_in_play.improvisation_reality: D&D is improvisational; game can go in unexpected directions

signal_mechanics.purpose: Allows players to communicate that a limit has been violated
signal_mechanics.no_debate: Signal shouldn't trigger debate or discussion

signal_implementation: Person invoking signal can comment on what they want adjusted
Person doesn't have to explain why content is objectionable
DM thanks player for being honest
DM sets scene right and moves on
alternative_approaches: Players can say 'stop' and pause game until issue is resolved
Players can step away from game if uncomfortable
Players can call for break to talk privately with DM
Players can give friend permission to use signal on their behalf
dm_modeling: Use every tool to adjust the shared story

intraparty_conflict.title: Intra-party Conflict
intraparty_conflict.overview: When characters in the party conflict, it signals one of three dynamics

three_causes.description: Player is exhibiting antisocial behavior in the game
Conflicts between characters surface conflicts between players
handling: Best handled away from gaming table
Encourage players to resolve conflict outside the game
If conflict keeps arising at table, ask players to take a break from campaign
May need to ask player to leave game entirely

examples: Disagreement on how to deal with captured enemy
Backing different sides in brewing war
handling: It's OK for characters and players to disagree
If disagreement gets too heated, take a break
Discuss out of character how players want to proceed
Find compromise that works for all

intraparty_conflict.identification: If you can't tell which dynamic is happening, have a conversation with the players

respect_for_players.title: Respect for the Players
respect_for_players.overview: Core principles for creating respectful gaming environment
player_expectations: Players need to know you'll run a fun, fair game tailored for them
You allow each to contribute to the story
You pay attention when they take their turns
You ensure adventure threats don't target them personally
dm_responsibility: Never make players feel uncomfortable or threatened
Ensure fair treatment for all players

do_you_really_do_that.title: Do You Really Do That? - Retracting Statements
do_you_really_do_that.overview: How to handle players who want to retract or change their character's actions

approaches.rule: If you said it, your character did it
approaches.effect: Makes players careful about what they say
approaches.downside: Can dampen atmosphere and discourage humor

approaches.rule: Players can freely retract or change their character's actions
Players can retract anything until they learn consequences of actions
implementation: Allow changes and retractions before consequences are described
Once DM describes what happens as a result, too late to change
benefits: Encourages humor and creativity
Maintains game pace
Prevents player frustration over misspoken words

sharing_the_spotlight.title: Sharing the Spotlight
sharing_the_spotlight.overview: Ensuring all players get equal attention and opportunity to participate
sharing_the_spotlight.core_principle: Don't play favorites or let one player dominate
dm_responsibility: Don't let one player do all the talking
Check in about what every character is doing
During exploration and social interaction, include all players
Don't focus just on one player's character
problematic_player_behavior: Tell other players what their characters should do
Claim best magic items for themselves

addressing_behavior.action: Point out that behavior is spoiling fun for others
addressing_behavior.approach: Ask player to tone it down

addressing_behavior.action: Ask player to leave the group

play_style_conflicts: Some players assume their style is superior to others
Impatience with encounters tailored to different preferences
Remedy: Remind impatient player (away from table) that you have a group to please, not just one

tragic_limits.title: Tragic Limits - Using Backstory in Campaign
tragic_limits.overview: Understanding player preferences for tragedy and involvement of beloved characters/places

two_player_types.tendency: Resist getting invested because they don't want to see loved things threatened/destroyed
two_player_types.need: Assurance that important backstory elements won't be destroyed

two_player_types.tendency: Gleefully create rich backstories expecting DM to use them as bait, victims, villains
two_player_types.need: Story opportunities to engage with their background

finding_balance: Don't alienate reluctant players by destroying what they love
Don't bore eager players by leaving their backstory out
using_threats_to_loved_ones: When antagonists threaten people/places characters care about
Ensure characters have chance to stave off worst outcome
Give characters opportunity to avoid or mitigate losses
Make tragedy consequence of character actions/decisions, not foregone conclusion
Moments of helplessness better suited for backstories

dm_die_rolling.title: DM Die Rolling - Visible vs Hidden
dm_die_rolling.overview: Choosing whether to roll dice openly or behind a screen

hidden_die_rolls.approach: Hide die rolls behind a DM screen
benefits: Keep rolls mysterious
Allows you to alter results if you want
Example: ignore Critical Hit to save character's life
caution: Never let players know when you fudge a roll

visible_die_rolls.approach: Roll dice in the open for all to see
benefits: Demonstrates impartiality
Shows you're not fudging to benefit or harm characters
Builds trust in fairness

hybrid_approach: Can make especially dramatic roll where everyone sees it

overly_cautious_players.title: Overly Cautious Players
overly_cautious_players.overview: Handling players who slow game by checking everything for traps

causes.description: Response to too many unpleasant surprises in past adventures
Manifestation of players' natural caution or anxiety
causes.recognition: Player tends toward careful play in general

in_game_techniques.description: Avoid traps and ambushes that feel random and have little importance
Set up situation where characters race toward goal or destination
in_game_techniques.caution: Use with caution; time pressure can increase player anxiety

in_game_techniques.description: Give players advance warning that encounter is imminent
in_game_techniques.benefit: Encourages players to move toward/away from encounters rather than anxiously anticipate ambush

meta_conversation: If in-game techniques don't work, have conversation outside game
Ask which game elements are causing cautious play
Come to agreement those elements won't appear
Explain: keeping game moving results in better experience

respect_for_dm.title: Respect for the DM
respect_for_dm.overview: As the DM, you have the right to expect respect from players
player_responsibility: Pay attention when DM narrates action
Respect the work DM puts into campaign

player_die_rolling.title: Player Die Rolling - Transparency
player_die_rolling.overview: Maintaining trust in die rolls by keeping them visible
player_die_rolling.standard: Players should roll dice in full view of everyone
enforcement: If player scoops up dice before anyone sees result, encourage transparency
Emphasize rolls should be visible to all
house_rules_for_lost_dice: When die falls on floor: do you count it or reroll?
When die lands cocked against object: pull object away or reroll?
Work with players to establish policy

social_contract_adventures.title: The Social Contract of Adventures
social_contract_adventures.overview: Mutual expectations between DM and players regarding adventure participation
dm_responsibility: Provide reasonably appealing reasons for characters to undertake adventures
Create compelling hooks that motivate party
player_responsibility: Go along with adventure hooks provided
Accept that DM has prepared content
allowable_player_input: Players can give pushback on adventure hooks
Players can ask why their characters should undertake adventure
DM can explain motivations and context
not_allowable: Willfully going in completely different direction
Invalidating the work DM put into adventure preparation
if_imbalance_occurs: Have conversation away from gaming table
Try to understand what hooks would motivate their characters
Explain the work you put into preparing adventures

rules_discussions.title: Managing Rules Discussions at the Table
rules_discussions.overview: Establishing group policy for how to handle rules questions

two_approaches.description: Group doesn't mind putting game on hold while discussing rule interpretations
Prefer DM to make call and continue playing

best_practice: Choose approach that works for your group
If glossing over rules issue, make note
Return to issue later when play has stopped
problematic_behavior: Some players use rules to argue against DM decisions
Can be helpful when DM makes simple mistake
Becomes problematic when done too often
Disrupts game flow and creates friction
handling_rule_lookup: If player wants to pause to find specific rule
Invite player to search while others continue
Player's character essentially steps out of game
In combat: character takes Dodge action
Other players keep playing instead of stopping entire game

character_knowledge.title: Maintaining Character vs Player Knowledge
character_knowledge.overview: Encouraging players to play within character knowledge limits
distinction_between_knowledge: Character knowledge: what your character knows
maintaining_distinction: Ask players: 'What do your characters think?'

anachronistic_thinking.description: Players using modern knowledge in fantasy world
remedy: Remind players things don't exist in game world
Modern science might not apply in fantasy

published_adventure_knowledge: Sometimes player knows Monster Manual thoroughly
Encourage separating player knowledge from character knowledge
Let other players discover things through play
Don't use meta-knowledge to optimize character builds unfairly

antisocial_behavior.title: Antisocial Behavior at the Table
antisocial_behavior.overview: Handling players who want to act out destructive or harmful fantasies
why_it_happens: Players play D&D because it lets them do things they can't in real life
Fight monsters, cast spells, explore worlds
For some, this becomes destructive: wreaking havoc, betraying allies
Not interested in heroic adventure, but in acting out harmful fantasies
when_to_address: If behavior appears, reopen conversation about game you want to play
Make clear what is and isn't acceptable
If just one player causing trouble, may need ultimatum
firm_stance: Out-of-control player who wants to continue must stop being disruptive
Must play as part of a team
Don't accept 'that's what my character would do' as excuse for jerk behavior
That excuse doesn't justify disrespecting other players

evil_characters.when_raised: If player asks to play evil character
what_to_do: Talk to player about what they have in mind
Make sure plans align with group expectations
legitimate_reasons: Some players want to explore playing evil for good reasons
Entire groups sometimes decide to play evil characters together
These are valid options if everyone's on same page
evil_characters.requirement: Everyone must agree on how campaign will go with evil characters

players_exploiting_rules.title: Players Exploiting the Rules
players_exploiting_rules.overview: Handling players who find rule combinations that break game balance
legitimate_vs_exploitative: Optimizing: playing with rules to find effective combinations
Part of the game, legitimate approach
Exploitative: using rule loopholes that interfere with fun

key_principles.explanation: Rules provide fun experience, not laws of physics
key_principles.example: Don't allow bucket brigade of people using Ready action to accelerate spear to light speed
key_principles.note: Ready action facilitates heroic action; doesn't define physical limitations

key_principles.principle: The Game Is Not an Economy
key_principles.explanation: Rules aren't intended to model realistic economy
key_principles.note: Don't allow spell combinations to generate infinite wealth

key_principles.explanation: Some rules only apply during combat or Initiative order
key_principles.enforcement: Don't let players attack each other or helpless creatures to activate rules

key_principles.explanation: Rules assume everyone interpreting them has group's fun at heart
key_principles.intent: Read rules with goal of fun in mind

when_to_address: Outline principles to hold exploits at bay
If player persistently tries to twist rules

knowing_the_rules.title: Knowing the Rules - You Don't Have to Be Perfect
knowing_the_rules.overview: Perspective on rules mastery and DM preparation
core_principle: You don't have to be expert on rules to be good DM
Facilitating fun is more important than implementing rules perfectly
if_unsure_about_rule: Ask opinion of players as a group
Usually takes a few minutes to reach answer
Fair answer more important than technically 'correct' answer
delegation: You don't need to know every spell
You don't need to know features of every class
Set expectation: players tell you what their abilities do
Players are responsible for knowing their own mechanics
resources: Use index to quickly find answers
It's fine to say 'let me look that up'

rules_virtual_table.title: Rules for the Virtual Table
rules_virtual_table.overview: Setting expectations for digital D&D gameplay
rules_virtual_table.same_principle: Setting expectations just as important digitally as in person

text_vs_voice.description: Confine out-of-character jokes, comments, memes to text channel
text_vs_voice.benefit: Keeps voice channel focused on game

text_vs_voice.description: Some groups find separate text distracting
text_vs_voice.consideration: Unfolding text conversation during game play distracts from action

rules_virtual_table.find_approach_that_works: Choose option best for your group

technical_decisions.decision: Who moves tokens on virtual tabletop?

options: Roll physical dice and report result
technical_decisions.guidance: Work with group to establish standard

guidance: Technology might dictate some answers
Sort out other questions as you play
Be flexible and willing to adjust
